questions.explanation: Synchronous code runs first (A, D), then microtasks like Promise callbacks (C), then macrotasks like setTimeout (B) — even with 0ms delay.

questions.question: What is the difference between named and default exports in ES modules?
options: Named exports use curly braces when importing; default exports don't
Default exports are faster to load
Named exports can only be functions; default exports can be anything
questions.answer: Named exports use curly braces when importing; default exports don't
questions.explanation: Named: import { add } from './math'. Default: import add from './math'. A module can have multiple named exports but only one default export.

questions.question: What is the Microtask Queue used for in the browser event loop?
questions.explanation: The Microtask Queue has higher priority than the Macrotask Queue and is fully drained after each task, before the next macrotask runs.

questions.question: What does 'type="module"' do when added to a script tag?
options: Enables ES module imports/exports and activates strict mode
Makes the script run before the DOM is ready
questions.answer: Enables ES module imports/exports and activates strict mode
questions.explanation: Script modules are deferred by default, have their own scope, are always in strict mode, and support import/export syntax.

questions.question: What is the advantage of dynamic imports (await import())?
options: They load modules faster than static imports
They allow conditional and lazy loading of modules, reducing initial bundle size
They allow importing from remote URLs
questions.answer: They allow conditional and lazy loading of modules, reducing initial bundle size
questions.explanation: Dynamic imports return a Promise and only load the module when called, enabling code splitting and lazy loading expensive libraries only when they're needed.
